definition: A structured list of components, subassemblies, and raw materials required to produce a finished product, including quantities and lead times. In planning software, the BOM drives how demand is exploded upstream through the supply chain.

Defines the distribution network: which locations supply which other locations, in what quantities, and with what lead times. The BOD drives how supply signals cascade downstream to distribution centres and customers.

A monthly cross-functional process that aligns demand, supply, inventory, and financial plans into a single agreed business plan. Typically runs over a multi-week cycle culminating in an executive review meeting.
A weekly operational process that monitors execution against the agreed S&OP plan, identifies short-term deviations, and triggers corrective actions. Sits between S&OP and day-to-day execution.
A buffer of inventory held above the expected demand to protect against variability in demand or supply lead times. Calculated per item-at-location and configured as a planning parameter.

The combination of a product (item) and a specific supply chain node (location). Most planning parameters — safety stock, lead time, reorder policies — are set at the item-at-location level rather than globally per item.
The input that drives the planning engine: typically customer orders, forecasts, or a combination of both. The demand signal is the starting point for all downstream supply calculations.

The output of the planning engine that tells each node in the supply chain what to produce, purchase, or transfer. Supply signals cascade upstream through the BOM and BOD in response to the demand signal.

A statistical or judgement-based estimate of future demand used as an input to the planning engine. Forecasts can be generated at different levels of aggregation (variety, item, location) and disaggregated for planning.

The process of splitting a high-level plan (e.g. an annual variety forecast) into lower-level detail (e.g. monthly item-at-location quantities). Planning software handles disaggregation automatically using configured ratios.
Splitting a single demand quantity across multiple time buckets or sources based on configured rules. Used to spread a lump-sum order or forecast across the planning horizon.
A netting logic where actual sales consume forecast in prior periods before consuming future forecast. Prevents double-counting of demand when orders arrive earlier than the forecast period.

An expected incoming supply — a purchase order, production order, or transfer order — that is already confirmed and dated. The planning engine nets scheduled receipts against demand before generating new supply proposals.

A supply strategy where production or procurement is triggered by a forecast, pushing supply down the chain before demand materialises. Suitable for long-lead-time items where waiting for actual orders is too late.
A supply strategy where production or procurement is triggered only by actual demand signals (orders). Reduces inventory risk but requires short enough lead times to respond in time.
A what-if version of the plan created by copying the current plan and applying changes. Simulations allow planners to explore scenarios without affecting the live plan, then promote the best outcome.

The system of record for transactional business data: purchase orders, production orders, inventory movements, and financials. The planning tool reads master data and actuals from the ERP and writes supply proposals back to it.

The process and tooling for creating, maintaining, and governing the core reference data used across systems — items, locations, BOMs, BODs, and resources. Clean master data is a prerequisite for accurate planning.

A responsibility assignment matrix defining who is Responsible, Accountable, Supporting, Consulted, and Informed for each activity in a process. Used in S&OP and S&OE to clarify cross-functional ownership.
The S&OP meeting (or pre-meeting) where commercial teams align on the consensus demand forecast. Output is a single agreed demand number used as the basis for supply planning.
The S&OP meeting where supply teams assess capacity and inventory against the agreed demand plan, identify gaps, and propose resolution options before the executive S&OP meeting.
A quantitative measure used to evaluate planning performance. Common planning KPIs include forecast accuracy, inventory days-of-supply, service level (fill rate), and planning cycle adherence.

A constrained capacity entity — typically a machine, line, or team — that the planning engine must schedule against. Resources have available capacity per time bucket and are consumed by production BOMs.

The maximum output a resource can produce within a given time bucket. The planning engine compares required load against available capacity to decide whether a demand plan is achievable, and flags overloads where it is not.

A supply plan calculated within the limits of available capacity and material, as opposed to an unconstrained plan that assumes infinite supply. The gap between the two reveals where capacity must be added or demand re-prioritised.
The resource or step with the least capacity relative to demand, which limits the throughput of the whole supply chain. Identifying and protecting the bottleneck is central to constrained planning.

The elapsed time between initiating a supply action — a purchase, production, or transfer — and that supply becoming available. Lead times are configured per item-at-location and drive how far ahead the engine must plan to meet demand on time.

A configurable setting that governs how the planning engine behaves for a given item-at-location — for example safety stock, lead time, reorder policy, lot size, or service-level target. Maintaining clean parameters is essential for trustworthy plan output.

The target probability of meeting demand from available stock without a stockout, often expressed as a percentage. Higher service-level targets require more safety stock, making this a key lever in the cost-versus-availability trade-off.

How long current inventory will last given projected demand, usually expressed in days or weeks of supply. Coverage analysis highlights where stock is at risk of running out or building up beyond target.

The calculation that subtracts on-hand inventory and scheduled receipts from gross demand to produce the net requirement the engine must plan new supply for. Netting is what turns a demand signal into actionable supply orders.

A forecast generated automatically from historical demand using statistical algorithms, before any human judgement is applied. It serves as the neutral starting point that commercial teams adjust during demand review.

The single agreed demand forecast produced when commercial teams overlay market intelligence — promotions, new products, customer plans — onto the statistical baseline. It is the demand number that drives supply planning.
How far into the future a plan extends. S&OP typically plans over a multi-month to multi-year horizon in monthly buckets, while S&OE works a short near-term horizon in weekly or daily buckets.

The unit of time into which a plan is divided — typically days, weeks, or months. Demand, supply, and capacity are aggregated per bucket; finer buckets give more precision at the cost of more data and volatility.
The rules that decide how constrained supply is distributed across competing demands — by customer priority, channel, region, or fair-share logic — when there is not enough to satisfy everyone.

An operating style where planners act only on items the system flags as outside tolerance — stockouts, overloads, plan deviations — rather than reviewing every record. It is the core working pattern of S&OE.

The defined path for raising a planning issue to a higher level of authority when it cannot be resolved within the normal process — for example a capacity shortfall that needs an executive decision on customer prioritisation.

Creating and comparing alternative versions of a plan under different assumptions — a demand surge, a supply disruption, a new capacity investment — to understand the impact before committing. Promising scenarios can be promoted to the operating plan.

The relatively stable reference data that describes the entities a business plans against — items, locations, BOMs, BODs, resources, and their parameters. Distinct from transactional data, it changes infrequently but underpins every calculation.

The high-volume, fast-changing records of business events — sales orders, purchase orders, goods movements, production confirmations. Transactional data flows from ERP into planning to reflect what is actually happening.

The principle that each data element has one authoritative, governed home that all systems and people reference, eliminating conflicting copies. It is the goal of master data management and a prerequisite for trusted plans.
A commitment to a supplier to deliver a specified quantity of an item by a date. Once raised it becomes a scheduled receipt that the planning engine counts as incoming supply.

An instruction to manufacture a specified quantity of an item by a date, consuming components per the BOM and capacity on a resource. It converts a planned supply recommendation into an executable shop-floor activity.

The transaction that records physical stock entering (goods receipt) or leaving (goods issue) a location, updating on-hand inventory in ERP. These movements feed the actuals that planning nets against.

A system that manages field-based operations — assignments, activities, yields, and actuals captured in the field — and feeds that data back into planning. Relevant where physical field work (e.g. agriculture or service) drives supply.
